sound_type: deep honk flock chorus -- two-syllable 'ah-HONK' at 200-2500 Hz, nasal brassy carrying, flock-amplified wall of sound, asynchronous overlapping honks, individual recognition by voice, E=5 collective power
ecosystem: Lake/grass, resident. Year-round resident populations in urban parks, lakeshores, golf courses, agricultural fields throughout the PNW. Migratory populations augment resident flocks in winter.
season: Year-round (resident populations) and winter visitor (migratory populations from Alaska/BC). V-formation migration peaks October-November and March-April. Honking is a 365-day phenomenon in the PNW.
family: Anatidae (12th consecutive: 118-129 -- RECORD EXTENDS TO TWELVE)

coordinates.description: Reference for Green Lake, Seattle -- one of the most prominent Canada Goose concentrations in urban PNW. Year-round resident flocks on the lake and surrounding grass.

taxonomy_note.description: TWELFTH consecutive Anatidae (118-129). Record extends. E=5 through collective flock power. First Branta (black goose) genus in the engine -- genus shift from Cygnus (swans, 127-128) to Branta (geese, 129+). Begins goose comparison pair with Cackling Goose (degree 130). Most ubiquitous waterfowl in North America.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Anatidae (12th consecutive -- RECORD EXTENDS, streak ongoing)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae: the most represented family. Twelve consecutive at degrees 118-129.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Branta from Old Norse brandgás ('burnt goose,' referring to dark plumage). canadensis from Latin 'of Canada.' The black-headed goose from Canada.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Branta -- first entry. The black goose genus. Genus shift from Cygnus (swans, 127-128) to Branta (geese, 129+).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes. Twelfth consecutive Anatidae. The family streak continues into goose territory.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at twelfth consecutive. ALL-TIME RECORD EXTENDS. Ducks (118-126), swans (127-128), geese (129+). All three major PNW Anatidae subfamilies represented.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 200-2,500 Hz. The fundamental is in the low range (200-500 Hz), with harmonics extending upward. Deeper honk in larger subspecies.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 300-1,000 Hz. The deep fundamental and first harmonics carry the most energy. The nasal, brassy quality comes from the syrinx structure.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Honk: 200-2,500 Hz. Alarm: sharp rapid series. Hiss: territorial defense. Gosling peep: high-pitched, whistle-like.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single honk: 0.5-1 second. Given continuously in flocks. V-formation honking is continuous overlapping calls.
song_metrics.note_structure: DEEP HONK: Two-syllable 'ah-HONK' with emphasis on second syllable. Nasal, brassy, carrying. Each individual distinct enough for voice recognition between mates and family.
song_metrics.note_types: Contact honk (primary, flock communication), alarm honk (sharp, rapid), territorial hiss (aggressive defense), pair greeting (mutual honking), gosling peep (high-pitched juvenile).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Asynchronous flock chorus. No coordinated timing. Each bird honks independently, creating continuous overlapping texture. V-formation adds Doppler shift during flyover.
song_metrics.timbre: Nasal. Brassy. Deep. Carrying. Less musical than Brant, less sharp than Cackling Goose. The rougher, more urgent quality of the flock chorus distinguishes it from swan baying.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Individual honk is moderate. Flock of 50-200 produces wall of sound. Collective power, not individual anatomy. Heard at 500+ meters single bird, 1-2 km flock.
song_metrics.tempo: Continuous in flocks. Each bird honks at own rate. Irregular, asynchronous rhythm creates characteristic textured wall of honking.
song_metrics.context: Flock communication, V-formation coordination, territorial defense, mate recognition, alarm signaling. The honk is multi-purpose: it is the Canada Goose's primary sound for all social contexts.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Varies by subspecies. B. c. maxima produces deeper honks. Smaller subspecies produce higher-pitched honks. Body size correlates directly with pitch.
song_metrics.key_property: DEEP HONK FLOCK CHORUS -- E=5 COLLECTIVE POWER. Two-syllable 'ah-HONK' at 200-2,500 Hz. Flock-amplified wall of sound. Individual recognition by voice. V-formation Doppler flyover. Most universally recognized waterfowl sound in N. America. TWELFTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-129). First Branta genus. Genus shift from Cygnus. Begins goose comparison pair with Cackling Goose (130). Seven subspecies. 2004 split created Cackling Goose. Resident populations 100K-200K+ in WA. Urban dominant.

plumage.overall: Black head and neck with distinctive white cheek patch (chinstrap). Brown-gray body. Black tail with white undertail coverts. Black bill, legs, and feet. Seven subspecies varying greatly in size.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: White cheek patch on black head and neck. Larger than Cackling Goose (130). Longer neck, larger bill, deeper honk. Body proportions distinguish from Cackling in the field.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Adults similar year-round. Juveniles slightly duller. Some rust staining on head/neck from feeding in iron-rich water.
plumage.flight_pattern: V-formation in flocks. Strong, steady wingbeats. Long neck extended. Continuous honking during flight. Doppler-shifted sound as formation passes overhead.

physical_description.overall: Body length 76-110 cm. Weight 2.6-6.5 kg (up to 14.3 lbs in B. c. maxima). Wingspan 127-185 cm. Enormous size variation across seven subspecies.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: White cheek patch. Black head and neck. Large size (vs. Cackling Goose). Longer neck and bill proportions.
physical_description.field_id: The white cheek patch on a black head is diagnostic. Distinguish from Cackling Goose (130) by larger size, longer neck, deeper honk, larger bill. In mixed flocks, size difference is apparent.

nodes.title: Deep Honk Flock Chorus -- E=5 Collective Power
nodes.description: Two-syllable 'ah-HONK' at 200-2,500 Hz. Flock-amplified. Asynchronous overlapping creates wall of sound. Individual recognition by voice. Most universally recognized waterfowl sound in North America. V-formation Doppler flyover is the iconic migration sound.

nodes.title: Urban Adaptation Specialist -- The Ubiquitous Giant
nodes.description: Resident populations 100K-200K+ in WA alone. Parks, golf courses, corporate campuses. Short grass near water = ideal habitat. Lost wariness of humans. Aggressive territorial defense. 500g droppings per bird per day. A victim of its own success -- so well-adapted it has become a management challenge.

nodes.title: V-Formation Aerodynamics and Acoustics
nodes.description: Upwash zone saves 10-14% flight energy. Leader rotation distributes fatigue. Honking coordination during flight. Doppler-shifted overhead passage is the iconic migration sound. First wild bird sound many PNW residents learn to identify.

nodes.title: Goose Comparison Pair Begins (129-130)
nodes.description: Canada Goose (129, large, deep honk, E=5, resident) vs. Cackling Goose (130, small, higher cackle, E=4, winter visitor). Split in 2004 by AOU. Fifth comparison pair in SPS. Mirrors Hairy/Downy WP (110-111), Barrow's/Common Goldeneye (123-124), Tundra/Trumpeter Swan (127-128).

nodes.title: Twelfth Consecutive Anatidae (118-129) -- Record Extends
nodes.description: Ducks (118-126), swans (127-128), geese (129+). All three PNW Anatidae subfamilies represented. Genus shift: Cygnus to Branta. Energy descends from E=6 climax (128) to E=5 (129). The streak enters goose territory. Twelve consecutive and counting.

cross_references.relationship: GOOSE PAIR BEGINS! Canada (129, deep honk, E=5, large, resident) vs. Cackling (130, higher cackle, E=4, small, winter visitor). Split in 2004. Identification challenge. Degree 130 completes the pair.
ENERGY ARC! Trumpeter (128, E=6 climax) to Canada Goose (129, E=5). Energy descends from maximum. Genus shift: Cygnus to Branta.
FLOCK ENERGY! Tundra (127, baying flock, E=5) and Canada Goose (129, honking flock, E=5). Both E=5 through collective power. Different sounds: melodious baying vs. brassy honking.
STREAK EXTENDED! Harlequin (118, E=5, stream) to Canada Goose (129, E=5, urban park). Twelve consecutive Anatidae. From torrential stream to urban landscape.
RECORD MARGIN GROWS! Picidae held 9 consecutive (107-115). Anatidae now at 12 (118-129). Three more than the previous record.
